race.name: White Mountain Classic
race.tagline: Thirty kilometers through the snow-draped White Mountains — New England Nordic racing at its finest.

vitals.aid_stations: Aid stations at regular intervals with warm drinks

climate.description: Jackson NH in late January is typically cold and snowy. Temperatures range from -15°C to -5°C. The White Mountains receive reliable snowfall, though freeze-thaw cycles can affect trail conditions. Elevation provides reasonable snow security.
challenges: New England freeze-thaw cycles can create icy conditions
Coastal storms can bring heavy wet snow

course.primary: Classic-only race through the Jackson XC trail network in the White Mountains of New Hampshire. The course weaves through birch and spruce forests with rolling terrain and scenic mountain views. Well-groomed trails with a mix of climbs and descents that reward good technique.
course.surface: Machine-groomed classic tracks through White Mountain forest
course.grooming: Well-maintained by Jackson XC ski touring foundation
features: Scenic White Mountain views throughout the course
Rolling forested terrain through birch and spruce
Classic technique only — rewards traditional skiing skills
Jackson XC has one of the most extensive trail networks in New England
Post-race gathering in the charming village of Jackson

nordic_lab_rating.score_note: A charming New England classic race with reliable snow and beautiful White Mountain scenery. Small field and limited reach keep it regional, but the setting and trail quality are strong.

history.summary: The White Mountain Classic grew out of the Jackson XC ski touring community, one of the oldest and most respected Nordic trail networks in New England. The race celebrates classic technique skiing through the White Mountains, drawing primarily from the New England skiing community. Jackson has been a hub for Nordic skiing since the 1970s.
notable_facts: Jackson XC operates one of the most extensive groomed trail networks in the northeastern US
The White Mountains are a traditional stronghold of New England Nordic skiing
Jackson village has a long history as a ski resort town dating to the early 20th century
Classic technique only — one of the few remaining technique-pure races in New England

youtube_data.search_query: White Mountain Classic cross country ski race Jackson

videos.title: Learning to Cross Country Ski at Jackson Ski Touring
videos.description: Christie takes a group lesson at Jackson Ski Touring Foundation. Jackson, NH is one of the top cross country ski destinations in the eastern United States.
videos.transcript:
[Music]
[Applause]
Jackson New Hampshire is famous for
cross-country skiing in shops and
restaurants connected by groomed trails
miles and miles more that extend through
woodlands over fields along rivers and
deep into the White Mountain National
Forest they beckon you to come explore
so that's what I'm going to do just one
problem
I've never cross-country ski today I'm
changing that by taking a lesson at the
Jackson ski touring foundation
so the lesson was a lot of fun and we
started off learning how to do weight
transference so pushing your weight onto
one ski and then the other and then we
learned a technique called double poling
and that's where you push off with both
poles you're bending the knees and
leaning forward and then lifting the
poles and getting a little momentum so
that was fun that was my first taste of
the speed with the poles they always
want to stay at an angle I don't one
rule of thumb is the bottom part of the
basket if you're starting to see it way
out in front of you you're bringing your
pole too far forward so poles are always
at an angle baskets out of sight I could
tuck my poles so I'm not tempted to do
anything like that really bend the knees
lean forward you can put your elbows on
your size if you want anywhere in this
zone is good just wherever you choose
stay with it
this tends to be a time people start to
creep up like this and then if you do
that you'll most definitely go over so
stay low and forward and keep your eyes
where you want to go and then we worked
our way up and down the hill and we
started learning how to walk up the hill
with the skis there's a piece of the ski
in the middle of the ski that has some
grip called fish skills and that's where
we got a little grip to get up the hill
and then on the way back down the hill
so that we didn't get too much speed we
tried a technique called snow plow where
you would put a triangle at your skis so
that you slowed down a little bit on the
way down and on the way up we also
learned the reverse triangle called
herringbone where you do a reverse
triangle and you edge into your skis so
that you get a feel for the snow and you
can kind of dig in a little bit going up
parallel or in the tracks I'm gonna
really try to keep my feet underneath me
tiny steps not as much glide as we had
on the flats reason for this there's
this middle section that's texture those
are called fish scales and that's what's
giving us grip going up there right
underneath our feet so that's why we
really want small steps keep your feet
right underneath you if you're starting
to push off too far back you're just
gonna get into this kind of slipping so
tiny steps as you move up keep your eyes
looking at the top of the hill
keep those poles at an angle this is
another time people start to bring them
too far
they're putting themselves back down and
then we started actually getting some
speed down the hill and that was a
little scary at first but it was a lot
of fun we got to try a little bit on the
tracks that felt super safe but going
down the hill tucking the poles under
again bending the knees leaning forward
and then if you were feeling like you
wanted to make a turn edging out with
one ski or the other to turn your body
weight your torso to one side or the
other as well nice I wasn't sure what to
expect for a lesson
I actually liked having it be a group
lesson but I could see the benefit to
both private or semi-private but it was
nice to have other people there so you
felt like you know wasn't just you the
novice and the instructor who was
experienced you had other people that
were learning with you so you're
learning from them and you're learning
with them my ski instructor was awesome
she was really nice she was very
friendly she made it fun and she wasn't
you know some instructors can make you
feel a little nervous but she didn't
make you feel that way at all she makes
you feel super comfortable and when I
followed right behind her it was really
easy to do much better on whatever
technique she was teaching me because
she's really good at what she does I
think if you know what you're doing it
may not come easily at the beginning but
at least you know how to correct
yourself and
having some knowledge behind it I think
helps you correct your mistakes and
enjoy the trip more oh absolutely I
think it just makes it easier because
then you have a foundation and you can
build on that foundation if you don't if
you're not taking a lesson then it's
just a video or just watching other
people and the instructors are terrific
Jenny's great just great just great so a
lot of fun stuff I learned I put it out
to the test on the trail and I didn't
fall Soho I think it was the success and
absolutely gorgeous day really great
company awesome instructor awesome
facility I can't say enough I had a
great time and I'm definitely coming
back for more for trail passes rentals
lessons and more visit the Jackson's eat
touring Center in Jackson New Hampshire
more info online at Jackson XT or

videos.title: Discover the joy of cross country skiing and snowshoeing at Jackson XC
videos.description: Jackson, NH is home to one of the most expansive trail networks in the east, all centered around an iconic New England village.
videos.transcript:

Trail networks and more than 10

Nordic ski conditions are incredible at Jackson Ski Touring in Jackson, NH. "Blue sky, powder snow, everything groomed, temps in the mid 20's" - Thom Perkins, JSTF

quotes.text: Jackson New Hampshire is famous for cross-country skiing in shops and restaurants connected by groomed trails miles and miles more that extend through woodlands over fields along rivers and deep into the White Mountain National Forest.
There's a piece of the ski in the middle of the ski that has some grip called fish scales and that's where we got a little grip to get up the hill.
